<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Calabi–Yau manifolds</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Calabi–Yau manifolds

Ricci-flat Kähler manifolds, the Hodge diamond, mirror symmetry, and what string theorists do at night.

blocks.html:
1. Definition: Ricci-flat Kähler manifolds

A Calabi–Yau manifold is a compact complex Kähler manifold $X$ of complex dimension $n$ satisfying any (hence all) of the following equivalent conditions:

  • The first Chern class vanishes: $c_1(X) = 0 \in H^2(X, \mathbb{R})$.
  • The canonical bundle is trivial: $K_X = \Omega^n_X \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
  • There is a nowhere-vanishing global holomorphic $n$-form $\Omega \in H^0(X, \Omega^n_X)$.
  • The holonomy of the Kähler metric is contained in $\mathrm{SU}(n)$ (rather than the larger $\mathrm{U}(n)$).

Some authors strengthen the definition by also requiring $h^{i,0}(X) = 0$ for $0 < i < n$, which excludes complex tori (so that ‘CY 3-fold’ in the strong sense means simply-connected with $\pi_1 = 0$). We use the broad definition unless noted.

The names attached to this object are not interchangeable. Calabi's conjecture (1954) asked whether every Kähler class on a manifold with $c_1 = 0$ contains a Ricci-flat representative. Yau's theorem (1976) proved it: there is a unique Ricci-flat Kähler metric in each Kähler cohomology class. The bridge from algebra ($c_1 = 0$, a topological cohomological condition) to geometry (an actual Einstein metric, $\mathrm{Ric} = 0$) is the central fact of the subject. Note: Ricci-flat is not flat — a K3 surface has nonzero Riemann curvature, only its trace vanishes.

Why degree $n+1$ in $\mathbb{P}^n$?
slide $n$ to see when adjunction makes a hypersurface CY

d = 5

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-cy-canonical");
  const out = $("#out-cy-canonical");
  const nEl = $("#cy-n"), dEl = $("#cy-d");
  const nValEl = $("#cy-n-val"), dValEl = $("#cy-d-val");

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const n = +nEl.value, d = +dEl.value;
    nValEl.textContent = "n = " + n;
    dValEl.textContent = "d = " + d;
    const k = d - n - 1; // K_X = O(d-n-1)|_X

    // axis showing K_X bundle line
    const cx = 60, cy = 110, w = 420;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:cx,y1:cy,x2:cx+w,y2:cy,stroke:"var(--line)","stroke-width":1}));
    // tick marks for k = -5..5
    for(let t=-5;t<=5;t++){
      const x = cx + w/2 + t*32;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x,y1:cy-5,x2:x,y2:cy+5,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1}));
      const tx = SVG("text",{x:x,y:cy+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      tx.textContent = t;
      svg.appendChild(tx);
    }
    const lbl = SVG("text",{x:cx+w/2,y:cy-32,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    lbl.textContent = "twist k of K_X = O(k)|_X";
    svg.appendChild(lbl);
    // colored zones
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:cx,y:cy-12,width:w/2 - 16,height:24,fill:"var(--cyan)","fill-opacity":0.15}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:cx+w/2+16,y:cy-12,width:w/2 - 16,height:24,fill:"var(--pink)","fill-opacity":0.15}));
    const tFano = SVG("text",{x:cx+w/4,y:cy-50,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"});
    tFano.textContent = "Fano (k < 0)";
    svg.appendChild(tFano);
    const tGT = SVG("text",{x:cx+3*w/4,y:cy-50,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--pink)"});
    tGT.textContent = "general type (k > 0)";
    svg.appendChild(tGT);
    const tCY = SVG("text",{x:cx+w/2,y:cy-50,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    tCY.textContent = "CY";
    svg.appendChild(tCY);

    // marker for current k
    const mx = cx + w/2 + Math.max(-5, Math.min(5, k))*32;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:mx,cy:cy,r:8,fill:k===0?"var(--yellow)":(k<0?"var(--cyan)":"var(--pink)"),stroke:"var(--ink)","stroke-width":1.5}));

    // verdict
    let verdict;
    if(k === 0) verdict = "CY: K_X trivial, holomorphic n-form Ω exists.";
    else if(k < 0) verdict = "Fano: K_X^{-1} ample, X positively curved on average.";
    else verdict = "general type: K_X ample, X is hyperbolic-ish.";

    out.textContent = `Smooth degree-${d} hypersurface X in P^${n} (dim X = ${n-1}).\n` +
      `Adjunction: K_X = O(d - n - 1)|_X = O(${k})|_X.\n` +
      `${verdict}`;
  }
  nEl.oninput = draw; dEl.oninput = draw;
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The widget above is the entire content of the adjunction formula for hypersurfaces: a smooth degree-$d$ hypersurface in $\mathbb{P}^n$ has $K_X = \mathcal{O}(d - n - 1)|_X$, and CY corresponds to the single equality $d = n+1$. This narrow window is why the cubic in $\mathbb{P}^2$ (genus 1), the quartic in $\mathbb{P}^3$ (K3), and the quintic in $\mathbb{P}^4$ (the canonical CY 3-fold) appear together — they are the Calabi–Yau hypersurfaces of dimensions 1, 2, 3.

See also
  • Kähler differentials $\Omega^1_{X/k}$
  • Differential forms and wedge product

</section>

2. The Hodge diamond

The cohomology of any compact Kähler manifold decomposes (Hodge decomposition) as $H^k(X, \mathbb{C}) = \bigoplus_{p+q=k} H^{p,q}(X)$, with $H^{p,q}(X) = H^q(X, \Omega^p_X)$. The numbers $h^{p,q} = \dim H^{p,q}$ form the Hodge diamond, displayed as a square rotated $45°$ with $h^{p,q}$ at position $(p, q)$. Two general symmetries hold for any Kähler manifold:

  • Hodge symmetry: $h^{p,q} = h^{q,p}$ (complex conjugation swaps the two summands).
  • Serre duality: $h^{p,q} = h^{n-p, n-q}$ (pair via $\wedge \Omega$ on a CY, or via $K_X$ in general).

For a CY $n$-fold, triviality of $K_X$ collapses Serre duality to the symmetry of the diamond about its centre. In addition, $h^{n,0} = h^{0,0} = 1$ (the holomorphic volume form $\Omega$ generates $H^0(K_X)$, and connectedness gives $h^{0,0} = 1$). Compactness plus connectedness force the corners. For a strict (simply-connected) CY 3-fold the diamond looks like:

Hodge diamond explorer

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-hodge-diamond");
  const out = $("#out-hodge");
  const nEl = $("#hd-n"), h11El = $("#hd-h11"), h21El = $("#hd-h21");
  const nValEl = $("#hd-n-val"), h11ValEl = $("#hd-h11-val"), h21ValEl = $("#hd-h21-val");
  const row11 = $("#hd-row-h11"), row21 = $("#hd-row-h21");
  const exBtns = $$("[data-ex]", $("#w-hodge-diamond"));

  function diamond(n, h11, h21){
    // returns h[p][q] for 0<=p,q<=n
    const h = Array.from({length:n+1}, ()=>Array(n+1).fill(0));
    // corners
    h[0][0] = h[n][n] = 1;
    h[n][0] = h[0][n] = 1;
    if(n === 1){
      // elliptic curve: 1, 1, 1, 1
      h[0][0]=1; h[1][1]=1; h[1][0]=1; h[0][1]=1;
    } else if(n === 2){
      // K3: 1; 0,0; 1,20,1; 0,0; 1
      // sliders interpret h11
      h[0][0]=1; h[2][2]=1; h[2][0]=1; h[0][2]=1;
      h[1][0]=0; h[0][1]=0; h[2][1]=0; h[1][2]=0;
      h[1][1]=h11;
    } else if(n === 3){
      h[0][0]=1; h[3][3]=1; h[3][0]=1; h[0][3]=1;
      h[1][0]=0; h[0][1]=0; h[3][2]=0; h[2][3]=0;
      h[2][0]=0; h[0][2]=0; h[3][1]=0; h[1][3]=0;
      h[1][1]=h11; h[2][2]=h11;
      h[2][1]=h21; h[1][2]=h21;
    }
    return h;
  }

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const n = +nEl.value, h11 = +h11El.value, h21 = +h21El.value;
    nValEl.textContent = "n = " + n;
    h11ValEl.textContent = h11;
    h21ValEl.textContent = h21;
    row21.style.display = (n === 3) ? "" : "none";
    row11.style.display = (n >= 2) ? "" : "none";

    const h = diamond(n, h11, h21);
    const cx = 260, cy = 180;
    const step = 56;
    // draw grid: each entry at (p-q, p+q) rotated 45°
    for(let p=0;p<=n;p++){
      for(let q=0;q<=n;q++){
        // diamond coordinates: x = (p-q)*step/2, y = ((p+q) - n)*step  (down = increasing degree)
        const x = cx + (p - q) * step;
        const y = cy + ((p + q) - n) * step / 1.2;
        const isCorner = (p===0&&q===0)||(p===n&&q===n)||(p===n&&q===0)||(p===0&&q===n);
        const isCentre = (p===Math.floor(n/2)&&q===Math.ceil(n/2)) || (p===Math.ceil(n/2)&&q===Math.floor(n/2));
        const fill = h[p][q]===0?"var(--panel2)":(isCorner?"var(--yellow)":(isCentre?"var(--violet)":"var(--cyan)"));
        const op = h[p][q]===0?0.3:1;
        const c = SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:y,r:18,fill,"fill-opacity":op,stroke:"var(--line)","stroke-width":1});
        svg.appendChild(c);
        const t = SVG("text",{x:x,y:y+5,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,"font-weight":600,fill:h[p][q]===0?"var(--mute)":"#111"});
        t.textContent = h[p][q];
        svg.appendChild(t);
        // small superscript label (p,q)
        const lab = SVG("text",{x:x,y:y-22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":9,fill:"var(--mute)"});
        lab.textContent = `(${p},${q})`;
        svg.appendChild(lab);
      }
    }

    // Euler characteristic
    let chi = 0;
    for(let p=0;p<=n;p++) for(let q=0;q<=n;q++) chi += ((p+q)%2===0?1:-1)*h[p][q];
    let bs = [];
    for(let k=0;k<=2*n;k++){
      let b = 0;
      for(let p=Math.max(0,k-n); p<=Math.min(n,k); p++) b += h[p][k-p];
      bs.push(b);
    }
    out.textContent = `n = ${n}. Betti numbers b_k for k=0..${2*n}: ${bs.join(", ")}.\n` +
      `Euler characteristic χ = ${chi}` +
      (n===3?` = 2(h¹¹ − h²¹) = 2(${h11} − ${h21}) = ${2*(h11-h21)}.`:".");
  }

  function applyEx(name){
    if(name === "quintic"){ nEl.value = 3; h11El.value = 1; h21El.value = 101; }
    else if(name === "mirror"){ nEl.value = 3; h11El.value = 101; h21El.value = 1; }
    else if(name === "K3"){ nEl.value = 2; h11El.value = 20; h21El.value = 0; }
    else if(name === "elliptic"){ nEl.value = 1; h11El.value = 1; h21El.value = 0; }
    exBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.toggle("active", b.dataset.ex === name));
    draw();
  }

  exBtns.forEach(b => b.onclick = () => applyEx(b.dataset.ex));
  nEl.oninput = h11El.oninput = h21El.oninput = () => { exBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.remove("active")); draw(); };
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Move the sliders to read off Betti numbers and Euler characteristic. The most loaded fact: for a CY 3-fold the diamond is determined by just two integers $h^{1,1}$ and $h^{2,1}$, with $\chi(X) = 2(h^{1,1} - h^{2,1})$. The mirror-pair button flips them — we will return to this in §5.

See also
  • Hodge numbers $h^{p,q}(X)$

Used in
Calabi–Yau manifolds · Mirror symmetry: swapping the diamond
Calabi–Yau manifolds · Periods and Picard–Fuchs
Mirror symmetry · The mirror pair statement
</section>

3. Examples: elliptic curves, K3, the quintic

The Calabi–Yau condition is restrictive enough that the early examples are isolated landmarks rather than a continuum.

Dimension 1: elliptic curves

A smooth projective curve has trivial canonical bundle iff $\deg K_C = 2g-2 = 0$, i.e. $g = 1$. Over $\mathbb{C}$ every such curve is a complex torus $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ — the simplest CY. There is a 1-parameter family parametrised by $\tau \in \mathbb{H}/\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, the moduli space of complex structures. See elliptic curves.

Dimension 2: K3 surfaces

The name is Kummer–Kähler–Kodaira (with a nod to the mountain). A K3 surface is a simply-connected compact complex surface with $K_X \cong \mathcal{O}_X$. The standard projective example is a smooth quartic in $\mathbb{P}^3$ — adjunction gives $K_X = \mathcal{O}(4-3-1) = \mathcal{O}$. All K3 surfaces are diffeomorphic; the Hodge diamond is fixed:

            1
          0   0
        1  20  1
          0   0
            1

and $\chi = 24$, $b_2 = 22$ (with intersection form $E_8(-1)^2 \oplus U^3$, the famous K3 lattice). Unlike elliptic curves, K3s come in a 20-dimensional moduli space of complex structures.

Dimension 3: the quintic and its zoo

The quintic 3-fold $\{f_5(x_0,\ldots,x_4) = 0\} \subset \mathbb{P}^4$ is the prototypical CY 3-fold, with Hodge numbers $(h^{1,1}, h^{2,1}) = (1, 101)$, hence $\chi = -200$. The 101-dimensional space of complex structures on the quintic is parameterised, modulo $\mathrm{PGL}_5$ action on the equation, by a $101$-dimensional variety. Other complete-intersection CY 3-folds in products of projective spaces give thousands of distinct Hodge-number pairs; even more come from hypersurfaces in toric Fano 4-folds (Batyrev–Borisov), whose enumeration produced the famous Kreuzer–Skarke list of $\sim$$473$ million reflexive 4D polytopes.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-cy-zoo");
  const out = $("#out-cy-zoo");
  const examples = [
    { id:"E", name:"Elliptic curve", eq:"cubic in P²", n:1, h:[[1,1],[1,1]], chi:0, mod:"j-invariant (1-dim)" },
    { id:"K3", name:"K3 surface", eq:"quartic in P³", n:2, h11:20, chi:24, mod:"20-dim complex structures" },
    { id:"Q", name:"Quintic 3-fold", eq:"deg 5 in P⁴", n:3, h11:1, h21:101, chi:-200, mod:"101-dim complex structures" },
    { id:"M", name:"Mirror quintic", eq:"orbifold of quintic", n:3, h11:101, h21:1, chi:200, mod:"1-dim complex structures" },
    { id:"CICY", name:"Complete intersection", eq:"e.g. (3,3) in P⁵", n:3, h11:1, h21:73, chi:-144, mod:"73-dim" },
    { id:"T", name:"Tian–Yau 3-fold", eq:"non-simply-connected", n:3, h11:14, h21:23, chi:-18, mod:"non-toric" }
  ];
  const cols = 3, rows = 2, w = 160, h = 110;

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    examples.forEach((ex, i) => {
      const r = Math.floor(i/cols), c = i%cols;

        "fill-opacity": sel ? 0.35 : 1,
        stroke: sel ? "var(--violet)" : "var(--line)",
        "stroke-width": sel ? 2 : 1});
      rect.style.cursor = "pointer";
      rect.onclick = () => { active = ex.id; draw(); };
      svg.appendChild(rect);
      const t1 = SVG("text",{x:x+w/2,y:y+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,"font-weight":600,fill:"#fff"});
      t1.textContent = ex.name;
      svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2 = SVG("text",{x:x+w/2,y:y+42,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      t2.textContent = ex.eq;
      svg.appendChild(t2);
      const t3 = SVG("text",{x:x+w/2,y:y+66,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--cyan)"});
      t3.textContent = "complex dim " + ex.n;
      svg.appendChild(t3);
      let bottom = "";
      if(ex.n === 3) bottom = "(h¹¹, h²¹) = (" + ex.h11 + ", " + ex.h21 + ")";
      else if(ex.n === 2) bottom = "h¹¹ = " + ex.h11 + ", χ = " + ex.chi;
      else bottom = "torus C/Λ";
      const t4 = SVG("text",{x:x+w/2,y:y+88,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
      t4.textContent = bottom;
      svg.appendChild(t4);
    });
    const ex = examples.find(e => e.id === active);
    if(ex.n === 3){
      out.textContent = `${ex.name} — ${ex.eq}.\n(h¹¹, h²¹) = (${ex.h11}, ${ex.h21}), χ = ${ex.chi}.\nModuli of complex structures: ${ex.mod}.`;
    } else if(ex.n === 2){
      out.textContent = `${ex.name} — ${ex.eq}.\nh¹¹ = ${ex.h11}, χ = ${ex.chi}, b₂ = 22.\nModuli: ${ex.mod}.`;
    } else {
      out.textContent = `${ex.name} — ${ex.eq}.\nDiamond: 1; 1, 1; 1. χ = 0. Moduli: ${ex.mod}.`;
    }
  }
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The pattern continues: in every dimension, hypersurfaces of degree $n+1$ in $\mathbb{P}^n$ give CY $(n-1)$-folds, and complete intersections of multidegrees $(d_1, \ldots, d_k)$ in $\mathbb{P}^{n}$ are CY iff $\sum d_i = n+1$. Toric methods extend this further. What is striking — and largely conjectural — is whether the list of distinct CY 3-folds is finite (Yau's finiteness conjecture). At present several hundred million reflexive polytope examples are known with no obvious upper bound.

See also
  • Bézout in higher dimensions
  • Riemann surfaces as 1D complex manifolds
</section>

4. Periods and Picard–Fuchs

Fix a CY $n$-fold $X_0$ and deform it through a family $\pi: \mathcal{X} \to S$ over a base $S$ (a disc $\Delta$ for now). Each fibre $X_t$ has a holomorphic $n$-form $\Omega_t$, well-defined up to scale, depending holomorphically on $t$. Pick a basis $\{\gamma_i\}$ of $H_n(X_0, \mathbb{Z})$ and parallel-transport it (using the Gauss–Manin connection on the local system $R^n\pi_* \mathbb{Z}$). The periods

$\Pi_i(t) = \displaystyle\int_{\gamma_i(t)} \Omega_t$

are multivalued holomorphic functions on $S \setminus (\text{discriminant})$. Differentiating $\Omega_t$ enough times against the connection produces a linear relation:

$\mathcal{L} \Pi(t) = 0, \qquad \mathcal{L} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^N a_k(t) \partial_t^k$.

This is the Picard–Fuchs equation. For the mirror quintic, written in a coordinate $z = (5\psi)^{-5}$, it is the order-$4$ hypergeometric operator

$\mathcal{L} = \theta^4 - 5z(5\theta + 1)(5\theta + 2)(5\theta + 3)(5\theta + 4), \qquad \theta = z\partial_z$,

and its four solutions encode the prepotential of the mirror $A$-model — the genus-$0$ Gromov–Witten generating function of the original quintic.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-period");
  const out = $("#out-period");
  const tauEl = $("#pd-tau"), tauValEl = $("#pd-tau-val");

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const tau = +tauEl.value / 100; // 0.2 to 3.0
    tauValEl.textContent = `τ = ${tau.toFixed(2)} i`;

    // Draw a torus = parallelogram lattice with A, B cycles
    const cx = 110, cy = 150;
    const ax = 120, ay = 0;       // A-cycle: real direction
    const bx = 0,   by = -ax * tau / 1.5;  // B-cycle: scaled by tau

    // parallelogram outline
    const pts = [
      [cx, cy],
      [cx + ax, cy + ay],
      [cx + ax + bx, cy + ay + by],
      [cx + bx, cy + by]
    ];
    const poly = SVG("polygon",{points: pts.map(p => p.join(",")).join(" "),
      fill:"var(--cyan)","fill-opacity":0.10,stroke:"var(--cyan)","stroke-width":1.5});
    svg.appendChild(poly);

    // A cycle (bottom edge) — yellow
    const aEdge = SVG("line",{x1:cx,y1:cy,x2:cx+ax,y2:cy+ay,stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":3});
    svg.appendChild(aEdge);
    const tA = SVG("text",{x:cx+ax/2,y:cy+18,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":14,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    tA.textContent = "A";
    svg.appendChild(tA);

    // B cycle (left edge) — pink
    const bEdge = SVG("line",{x1:cx,y1:cy,x2:cx+bx,y2:cy+by,stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":3});
    svg.appendChild(bEdge);
    const tB = SVG("text",{x:cx+bx-14,y:cy+by/2,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":14,fill:"var(--pink)"});
    tB.textContent = "B";
    svg.appendChild(tB);

    // Now draw the period vector (Pi_A, Pi_B) in C
    // For an elliptic curve C/(Z + tau Z), Omega = dz, integrals:
    //   int_A dz = 1, int_B dz = tau
    const px = 350, py = 80, scale = 60;
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:px-80,y1:py+90,x2:px+150,y2:py+90,stroke:"var(--line)"}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:px,y1:py-30,x2:px,y2:py+180,stroke:"var(--line)"}));
    const axL = SVG("text",{x:px+155,y:py+95,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    axL.textContent = "Re";
    svg.appendChild(axL);
    const ayL = SVG("text",{x:px+5,y:py-30,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    ayL.textContent = "Im";
    svg.appendChild(ayL);

    // origin
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:px,cy:py+90,r:3,fill:"var(--mute)"}));

    // Pi_A = 1
    const aend = [px + scale * 1, py + 90 - 0];
    drawArrow(svg, [px, py+90], aend, {color:"var(--yellow)", label:"∫_A Ω = 1", markerId:"pd-arr-y"});

    // Pi_B = tau (pure imaginary)
    const bend = [px + 0, py + 90 - scale * tau];
    drawArrow(svg, [px, py+90], bend, {color:"var(--pink)", label:"∫_B Ω = τ", markerId:"pd-arr-p", labelSide:0});

    // Picard-Fuchs note
    const tNote = SVG("text",{x:px-80,y:py+220,"font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    tNote.textContent = "PF: τ̇ = 0 (trivial here)";
    svg.appendChild(tNote);

    out.textContent = `Holomorphic 1-form Ω = dz on C/(Z + τZ).\n` +
      `Periods: ∫_A Ω = 1, ∫_B Ω = τ = ${tau.toFixed(3)}i.\n` +
      `The pair (∫_A, ∫_B) varies holomorphically with τ — the Picard–Fuchs equation here is just dτ/dτ = 1.\n` +
      `For the quintic family, the analogous 4 periods satisfy a hypergeometric ODE of order 4.`;
  }
  tauEl.oninput = draw;
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The picture above is the genus-1 cartoon: as $\tau$ varies on the right, the elliptic curve on the left changes shape, and the period $\int_B \Omega = \tau$ encodes that shape. For a CY 3-fold the analogous picture is 4-dimensional (one $\Omega$, four cycles), the Picard–Fuchs ODE is order 4, and its monodromy at the discriminant punctures of moduli encodes the entire variation of Hodge structure — and through mirror symmetry, all of the curve-counting on the mirror.

See also
  • The algebraic de Rham complex $\Omega^\bullet_{X/k}$
  • Čech cocycles and cohomology

5. Mirror symmetry: swapping the diamond

Mirror symmetry is the conjecture (now theorem in many cases) that CY 3-folds come in pairs $(X, Y)$ such that

$h^{1,1}(X) = h^{2,1}(Y), \qquad h^{2,1}(X) = h^{1,1}(Y)$,

and physical / mathematical structures on $X$ are interchanged with structures on $Y$:

$A$-model on $X$$\leftrightarrow$$B$-model on $Y$
Kähler moduli ($h^{1,1}$ many)=complex-structure moduli ($h^{2,1}$ many)
Symplectic structure=Complex structure
Gromov–Witten invariants (curve counts)=Periods of $\Omega$
Quantum cohomology=Variation of Hodge structure
Type IIA superstring on $X$=Type IIB superstring on $Y$

Historically: in 1991 Candelas, de la Ossa, Green, and Parkes computed the prepotential of the mirror quintic from periods of the hypergeometric Picard–Fuchs equation, expanded the answer near the large-volume limit, and read off predictions for the number $n_d$ of rational curves of each degree on a generic quintic 3-fold:

$n_1 = 2{,}875, \quad n_2 = 609{,}250, \quad n_3 = 317{,}206{,}375, \quad n_4 = 242{,}467{,}530{,}000, \ldots$

The case $n_1 = 2{,}875$ matched a 19th-century enumerative calculation of Schubert. The case $n_2 = 609{,}250$ corrected an algebraic-geometry computation that had stood since the 1980s. Higher $n_d$ were unprovable by any then-existing method. Givental and Lian–Liu–Yau later proved the predictions rigorously.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-mirror");
  const out = $("#out-mirror");
  const tEl = $("#mr-t"), tValEl = $("#mr-t-val");

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const t = +tEl.value / 100;
    tValEl.textContent = t === 0 ? "t = 0 (X = quintic)" : (t === 1 ? "t = 1 (Y = mirror)" : `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`);

    // Two diamonds, X on left, Y on right, with the central row interpolating
    function diamondAt(cx, cy, h11, h21, label, color){
      const step = 28;
      // Positions for CY 3-fold diamond (n=3)
      const positions = [
        // [p, q, value]
        [0,0,1], [1,0,0], [2,0,0], [3,0,1],
        [0,1,0], [1,1,h11], [2,1,h21],
        [0,2,0], [1,2,h21], [2,2,h11],
        [0,3,1], [3,1,0], [3,2,0], [3,3,1], [1,3,0], [2,3,0]
      ];
      positions.forEach(([p,q,v]) => {
        const x = cx + (p - q) * step;
        const y = cy + ((p + q) - 3) * step;
        const isCorner = (v === 1 && (p === 0 || p === 3));
        const fill = v === 0 ? "var(--panel2)" : (isCorner ? "var(--yellow)" : color);
        const op = v === 0 ? 0.3 : 0.85;
        svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:y,r:14,fill,"fill-opacity":op,stroke:"var(--line)"}));
        const tx = SVG("text",{x,y:y+4,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,"font-weight":600,fill:v===0?"var(--mute)":"#111"});
        tx.textContent = v;
        svg.appendChild(tx);
      });
      const lab = SVG("text",{x:cx,y:cy+90,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":13,"font-weight":600,fill:"#fff"});
      lab.textContent = label;
      svg.appendChild(lab);
    }

    // Interpolate h11 / h21 across the diamond pair
    const xH11 = Math.round(1 + 100 * t);     // 1 -> 101
    const xH21 = Math.round(101 - 100 * t);   // 101 -> 1
    diamondAt(140, 110, xH11, xH21, t < 0.5 ? "X (quintic)" : "→ becoming mirror Y", "var(--cyan)");
    diamondAt(380, 110, 101 - Math.round(100*t), 1 + Math.round(100*t), t < 0.5 ? "Y (mirror quintic)" : "→ becoming X", "var(--violet)");

    // Big arrow between
    drawArrow(svg, [220, 110], [300, 110], {color:"var(--yellow)", curve: 18, label:"mirror", markerId:"mr-arr-1"});
    drawArrow(svg, [300, 130], [220, 130], {color:"var(--yellow)", curve: 18, label:"map", markerId:"mr-arr-2", labelSide:0});

    out.textContent = `Mirror swap of (h¹¹, h²¹).\n` +
      `Currently rendering X with (${xH11}, ${xH21}); Y with (${101 - Math.round(100*t)}, ${1 + Math.round(100*t)}).\n` +
      `χ(X) = 2(h¹¹ − h²¹) = ${2*(xH11 - xH21)}; χ(Y) = ${2*((101-Math.round(100*t))-(1+Math.round(100*t)))}.\n` +
      `So χ(X) = −χ(Y), as it must under mirror symmetry.`;
  }
  tEl.oninput = draw;
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Kontsevich's homological mirror symmetry proposal upgrades the Hodge-number swap to an equivalence of categories: $D^b \mathrm{Coh}(X) \simeq D^\pi \mathrm{Fuk}(Y)$, the bounded derived category of coherent sheaves on $X$ matches the (split-closed) derived Fukaya category on $Y$. This has been proved for many examples (Seidel for the genus-2 surface and quartic surface; Polishchuk–Zaslow for elliptic curves; Sheridan for Fano hypersurfaces and CY hypersurfaces in projective space).

6. String compactifications, DT, SYZ

String theory

The original motivation: superstring theory wants a 10-dimensional spacetime, but only 4 are observed. The compactification $\mathbb{R}^{1,3} \times X^6$ preserves $\mathcal{N} = 1$ supersymmetry in the effective 4D theory iff the Killing spinor equation $\nabla \xi = 0$ admits a covariantly constant spinor on $X$ — equivalently, $X$ has special holonomy contained in $\mathrm{SU}(3) \subset \mathrm{SO}(6)$. That is exactly the CY condition. Moduli of the CY (its $h^{1,1} + h^{2,1}$ shape parameters) become massless scalar fields ("moduli fields") in 4D, and engineering models of particle physics from CY compactification is a major industry — the string landscape.

Donaldson–Thomas invariants

For a CY 3-fold $X$, the moduli space of stable ideal sheaves $\mathcal{I}$ with fixed Chern character carries a perfect obstruction theory of virtual dimension 0; integrating $1$ against the virtual fundamental class gives an integer, the Donaldson–Thomas invariant $\mathrm{DT}(X, \beta, n)$. The MNOP conjecture (Maulik–Nekrasov–Okounkov–Pandharipande, partly proved) says these are equivalent to Gromov–Witten invariants of $X$ via an explicit generating-function identity, exhibiting a stable-pairs / curve-counting correspondence. Under mirror symmetry, DT on $X$ corresponds to GW on $Y$.

Strominger–Yau–Zaslow

The geometric explanation for why mirror pairs exist at all: SYZ (1996) conjectures that a CY 3-fold $X$ near a maximally-unipotent boundary point of moduli admits a special-Lagrangian $T^3$-fibration $X \to B$ over a 3-base with codimension-2 discriminant locus. The mirror $Y$ is obtained by dualising the torus fibres ($T$-duality on each $T^3$). Any local fibrewise calculation on $X$ can be transported to a calculation on $Y$ — the swap of $h^{1,1}$ and $h^{2,1}$ comes from swapping the cohomology of the base with the cohomology of the fibre (via the Leray spectral sequence). SYZ has been established near the large-complex-structure limit by Gross–Wilson and developed rigorously in the toric/tropical setting by Gross–Siebert.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#svg-syz");
  const out = $("#out-syz");
  const bEl = $("#syz-b"), bValEl = $("#syz-b-val");

  function torus(cx, cy, scale, color, fill){
    const g = SVG("g");
    // Outer ellipse
    const outer = SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:scale*1.2,ry:scale*0.55,fill,"fill-opacity":0.18,stroke:color,"stroke-width":1.5});
    g.appendChild(outer);
    // Inner ellipse (hole)
    const inner = SVG("ellipse",{cx,cy,rx:scale*0.55,ry:scale*0.20,fill:"var(--bg)",stroke:color,"stroke-width":1.2});
    g.appendChild(inner);
    return g;
  }

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    const b = +bEl.value / 100;
    bValEl.textContent = `b = ${b.toFixed(2)}`;

    // Base interval B from x=60 to x=460, y=240
    const baseY = 240;
    const x0 = 60, x1 = 460;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x0,y1:baseY,x2:x1,y2:baseY,stroke:"var(--blue)","stroke-width":2}));
    const tBase = SVG("text",{x:x1+10,y:baseY+4,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--blue)"});
    tBase.textContent = "B";
    svg.appendChild(tBase);

    // Discriminant points (where torus fibre degenerates)
    const discPts = [0.20, 0.55, 0.85];
    discPts.forEach(d => {
      const x = x0 + d*(x1-x0);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:baseY,r:5,fill:"var(--pink)",stroke:"var(--ink)","stroke-width":1}));
    });
    const tDisc = SVG("text",{x:(x0+x1)/2,y:baseY+24,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--pink)"});
    tDisc.textContent = "discriminant locus (singular fibres)";
    svg.appendChild(tDisc);

    // Currently selected base point
    const xb = x0 + b*(x1-x0);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:xb,cy:baseY,r:7,fill:"var(--yellow)",stroke:"var(--ink)","stroke-width":2}));

    // Determine if near discriminant
    const minDist = Math.min(...discPts.map(d => Math.abs(b - d)));
    const isSingular = minDist < 0.04;

    // Draw fibre torus floating above
    const fcx = xb, fcy = 110;
    const scale = 50;
    if(isSingular){
      // pinched torus - draw circle with X
      const c = SVG("circle",{cx:fcx,cy:fcy,r:scale*0.6,fill:"var(--pink)","fill-opacity":0.18,stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2});
      svg.appendChild(c);
      const x1p = SVG("line",{x1:fcx-scale*0.4,y1:fcy-scale*0.4,x2:fcx+scale*0.4,y2:fcy+scale*0.4,stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2});
      svg.appendChild(x1p);
      const x2p = SVG("line",{x1:fcx+scale*0.4,y1:fcy-scale*0.4,x2:fcx-scale*0.4,y2:fcy+scale*0.4,stroke:"var(--pink)","stroke-width":2});
      svg.appendChild(x2p);
      const tF = SVG("text",{x:fcx,y:fcy-scale-10,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--pink)"});
      tF.textContent = "singular fibre (pinched T³)";
      svg.appendChild(tF);
    } else {
      // healthy T^3 — draw two stacked tori suggestion
      svg.appendChild(torus(fcx, fcy + 20, scale*0.9, "var(--cyan)", "var(--cyan)"));
      svg.appendChild(torus(fcx, fcy - 5, scale*0.7, "var(--cyan)", "var(--cyan)"));
      const tF = SVG("text",{x:fcx,y:fcy-scale-12,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--cyan)"});
      tF.textContent = "fibre T³ (smooth)";
      svg.appendChild(tF);
    }

    // dashed line from base to fibre
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:xb,y1:baseY-8,x2:fcx,y2:fcy+scale*0.6,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1,"stroke-dasharray":"4,3"}));

    out.textContent = `CY 3-fold X → B is a special-Lagrangian T³-fibration.\n` +
      `Base point b = ${b.toFixed(2)}: ${isSingular ? "near discriminant — fibre degenerates (pinched T³)." : "smooth fibre T³ = R³/Z³."}\n` +
      `Mirror Y is obtained by dualising each T³ fibrewise (T-duality).`;
  }
  bEl.oninput = draw;
  draw();
})();
</script>

Connections

Calabi–Yau geometry has been central to algebraic and differential geometry since Yau's 1976 proof, and central to physics since the 1985 paper of Candelas–Horowitz–Strominger–Witten on string compactification. The open frontiers are still vast: classification of CY 3-folds (Yau's finiteness conjecture remains open), homological mirror symmetry beyond the Fano / hypersurface cases, the SYZ programme over a generic base, and the role of CY $n$-folds for $n \geq 4$ in F-theory.

  • Differential forms — Kähler differentials — the holomorphic $n$-form $\Omega$ that defines a CY lives in $H^0(X, \Omega^n_X)$.
  • Algebraic de Rham — Hodge numbers — the Hodge diamond of §2 and Serre duality are special cases of the Hodge / Lefschetz package.
  • Elliptic curves — the dimension-1 case, where the period $\tau$ already exhibits the modular features that generalise to higher-dimensional CY.
  • Moduli spaces — the $h^{1,1}$ Kähler moduli and $h^{2,1}$ complex-structure moduli are tangent directions to families of CY, and their global geometry is largely uncharted.
  • Mirror symmetry — the dedicated topic that picks up where §5 leaves off, including homological mirror symmetry and the SYZ programme.
